q: In a Databricks SQL environment, when should analysts use Genie versus the SQL editor in practice?
a: Genie and the Databricks SQL editor serve different parts of the analyst's day — they're not competing, they're complementary. Use Genie for: exploring a domain you understand conceptually but haven't written SQL for yet; answering one-off questions from stakeholders that don't justify a new dashboard or saved query; letting a non-SQL business user self-serve a question without analyst intervention; getting a first-draft SQL you can then open in the editor and refine. Use the SQL editor for: queries you'll run on a schedule or pin to a dashboard; complex queries you need to optimize (check the query plan, add hints, test partitioning); queries involving CTEs, window functions across many stages, or Spark-specific syntax that Genie may not generate optimally; debugging and iterating when you know the schema well and want direct control. The critical insight for senior engineers: Genie's value is in freeing analyst time, not replacing SQL expertise. The analyst's role shifts from writing every query to curating the Genie Space quality — writing SQL expressions, verifying example queries, and running benchmarks — so the Space stays accurate for the business users who rely on it. Using Genie for a question where you know the exact SQL is wasted latency; using the SQL editor for a domain question you'd spend 30 minutes exploring is wasted time.
fj: Genie will replace the SQL editor for most analyst work — why write SQL if the AI can do it?
fs: Genie is for exploration and non-SQL users; the SQL editor is for precision, optimization, and scheduled queries. For senior analysts the real shift is curation: your value is in the Space quality (SQL expressions, example queries, benchmarks) that makes Genie accurate for everyone else.
